prompt_md: What is the key difference between `std::optional<T>` and `std::expected<T, E>`?

options.a: std::optional can hold any type; std::expected only holds integers.
options.b: std::optional signals value-or-nothing; std::expected signals value-or-a-specific-error, preserving the reason for failure.
options.c: std::expected is faster because it avoids heap allocation.
options.d: std::optional is for runtime errors; std::expected is for compile-time errors.

explanation_md: `std::optional` models absence — it tells you *whether* a value exists but not *why* it is missing. `std::expected<T, E>` models either a success value of type `T` or an error value of type `E`, preserving the error reason. Use `optional` when absence is normal and unremarkable; use `expected` when callers must handle or propagate error information.

prompt_md: What does `std::println("value: {}", x)` do compared to `std::cout << "value: " << x << "\n";`?

options.a: They are identical — println is just an alias for cout.
options.b: println uses the std::format syntax for type-safe formatted output and automatically appends a newline, making the call more concise.
options.c: println writes to stderr; cout writes to stdout.
options.d: println is slower because it allocates a temporary string.

explanation_md: `std::println` combines `std::format`-style type-safe placeholder formatting with automatic newline appending. It is more concise than chaining `cout` insertions and safer than `printf` (wrong argument types are compile errors). Both write to stdout by default.

prompt_md: What is the purpose of `std::ranges::to<std::vector>()` in C++23?

options.a: It converts a std::vector into a raw array.
options.b: It materializes a lazy range view into an owned std::vector container.
options.c: It sorts a range and stores the result in a new vector.
options.d: It reserves capacity in an existing vector.

explanation_md: Range views in C++20 are lazy — they do not allocate storage. `std::ranges::to<std::vector>()` iterates the view and constructs an owned `std::vector` from its elements. This is the idiomatic C++23 way to bridge lazy range pipelines and code that needs a concrete container.

prompt_md: You call `auto r = some_expected_fn();` and `r` holds an error. What happens if you write `int val = *r;`?

options.a: val is set to 0, the default for int.
options.c: It is undefined behavior — dereferencing a failed std::expected has no defined outcome.
options.d: The program prints the error message and continues.

explanation_md: Dereferencing a failed `std::expected` with `operator*` is undefined behavior. If you want an exception on failure, call `.value()` instead — it throws `std::bad_expected_access<E>`. Always check `if (r)` or use `.value()` rather than `*r` when you have not verified that the result holds a value.
